figures.caption: Fig. 1: Primal-dual expert policies. Top-left panel shows the evolution of the dual multipliers associated with the ergodic minimum-rate constraints of selected receivers for an example network H in the power-control problem of Section 3. The golden shaded region marks the convergence regime. Bottom-left panel plots the per-iteration fraction of violated constraints against the worst-case constraint slack, averaged over a 2000-iteration moving window. Although the iterates x†(λk) remain infeasible for at least 20% of the receivers, the randomized policy D̂x(· |H) is near-optimal and feasible in the average-sense [cf. (11), (12)]. The long transient preceding convergence is evident from both panels. The panels on the right show several 2D slices of the allocation vectors drawn from the expert policy in the convergence regime, with samples from the transient regime shown as faint markers. The top-row slices correspond to the receivers plotted in the top-left panel. The orange overlays show the corresponding slices for samples generated by the learned policy Dx(· |H;θ∗), which closely matches the experts.

Fig. 2: Performance of the time-shared U-GNN policy. The U-GNN policy closely tracks the expert policy in terms of tail rates (left, middle) while achieving comparable mean-rate utility (right). The deterministic baselines fail to satisfy the QoS levels for the tail users.

Fig. 4: Size-transferability of U-GNN policies. The model is trained on networks of size N = 400 and four density levels ν ∈ {6.6, 8.2, 10.1, 11.9} tx-rx pairs/km2, and evaluated on networks of varying sizes at similar densities.
Fig. 3: Generalization of U-GNN policies across fmin (QoS) levels. The model is trained on fmin ∈ {0.4, 0.5, 0.6, 0.7, 0.8} and evaluated at intermediate fmin levels.
